why: The chain of events that took Anthropic's two most capable models offline, from Amazon's internal guardrail-bypass disclosure to a Commerce Department ban on foreign nationals, sets a concrete policy precedent for how frontier model access can be severed across an entire deployment ecosystem overnight.
notes: SK Telecom was among roughly 150 organizations added to Project Glasswing, Anthropic's Mythos vulnerability-detection distribution program, in early June; the White House requested its access be revoked shortly after, citing parent SK Group's China semiconductor and energy interests rather than SK Telecom's own minimal direct exposure ($1.9M in Chinese revenue and 7 employees in China in 2024).

SK Telecom's historical China entanglement includes a 2004 wireless joint venture (UNISK) with state-owned China Unicom, a $1B convertible-bond investment in 2006 that converted to a roughly 6.6% stake and was sold in 2009 for $1.3B; a residual UNISK investment of approximately $17M still appeared in SK Telecom's 2025 SEC filing.

SK Hynix and Samsung received Mythos access in the same Project Glasswing expansion and are both strategic investors in Anthropic, as well as two of the largest suppliers of memory and logic silicon underpinning AI hardware, creating a structural conflict between U.S. supply-chain dependency and the export-control rationale.

The June 12 Commerce Department ban originated from a separate incident: Amazon, Anthropic's largest investor (cumulative stake roughly $13B), flagged a Fable 5 guardrail bypass to the White House after researchers demonstrated the model reading a codebase and patching its own vulnerabilities as a vulnerability-discovery tool; CEO Andy Jassy reportedly raised the finding directly with administration officials.

Anthropic disabled Mythos 5 and Fable 5 entirely rather than implement nationality-based filtering; roughly 100 cybersecurity professionals, including former Facebook security chief Alex Stamos and Luta Security's Katie Moussouris, signed an open letter arguing these models are 'quite good' at finding and weaponizing software flaws but 'are not uniquely good,' and that the controls would harm industry-wide deployments.
summary: Wired identified SK Telecom, South Korea's largest wireless carrier, as the organization whose access to Anthropic's Claude Mythos model was revoked by White House request in early June, with the stated security concern attaching to parent SK Group's China semiconductor and energy interests rather than to SK Telecom's own minimal Chinese footprint. The situation is structurally tangled because SK Hynix, a sibling company within SK Group, received Mythos access in the same Project Glasswing expansion alongside Samsung, and both companies serve simultaneously as Anthropic strategic investors and as major suppliers of the memory and logic silicon the AI hardware stack depends on. The broader export controls arose from a distinct incident: Amazon flagged a Fable 5 guardrail bypass to the White House after researchers used the model as a self-directed vulnerability-discovery tool, and Amazon CEO Andy Jassy escalated the finding directly to administration officials, prompting the Commerce Department's June 12 order barring foreign nationals and U.S. immigrants from accessing Fable 5 and the underlying Mythos 5. Rather than filter by nationality, Anthropic disabled both models universally and characterized the demonstrated capability as surfacing only a small number of previously known, minor vulnerabilities. Approximately 100 cybersecurity professionals, including Alex Stamos and Katie Moussouris, published an open letter contending the models are not uniquely capable at vulnerability discovery and that the controls would halt industry-wide deployments. Both models remain offline as negotiations with the White House continue, even as Anthropic opened a Seoul office on June 17 and named SK Telecom as a partner in a memorandum of understanding with South Korea's Ministry of Science and ICT.
novelty: SK Telecom's identification as the revoked organization; timeline showing Amazon-triggered export control preceded broader White House ban by days; Samsung/SK Hynix dual role as chip suppliers and strategic investors facing policy blowback
audience: AI policy observers, Anthropic stakeholders, semiconductor supply-chain analysts tracking AI hardware dependency

title: Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe
why: The E2M1 FP4 format hardwired into NVIDIA Blackwell and AMD MI350 accelerators has a geometric flaw that systematically degrades pretraining quality, and this paper names the mechanism, quantifies the damage, and proposes a drop-in recipe fix before the hardware is even widely deployed.
notes: E2M1's non-uniform representable bins create what the authors call Shrinkage Bias: a systematic negative rounding error that accumulates multiplicatively across transformer layers, not merely additively.

Random Hadamard Transform (RHT), widely applied to improve quantization bucket utilization, paradoxically amplifies shrinkage bias in E2M1; the authors offer this as a unified mechanistic explanation for the training instability observed in existing E2M1-based FP4 recipes.

UFP4 sidesteps the problem by switching to uniform E1M2/INT4 grids, applying RHT to all three training GEMMs (forward, weight gradient, activation gradient), and restricting stochastic rounding to dY (the activation gradient) only.

Validation spans Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining; UFP4 achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than E2M1 baselines at all three scales, with scaling-law analysis and ablation studies included.

The authors explicitly state that current NVIDIA Blackwell/Rubin-class and AMD MI350 hardware training paths are centered on E2M1, and recommend future accelerators add E1M2/INT4-style uniform grids as first-class training primitives alongside it.
summary: This paper identifies Shrinkage Bias as a fundamental limitation of E2M1, the FP4 format at the center of current training paths on NVIDIA Blackwell, Rubin-class, and AMD MI350 GPUs. The bias arises from the geometric asymmetry of E2M1's non-uniform representable bins, producing a systematic negative rounding error that compounds multiplicatively across layers rather than canceling. The authors further show that Random Hadamard Transform, commonly used to improve quantization bucket utilization, amplifies this bias rather than mitigating it, providing a mechanistic account of previously observed E2M1 training instability. In response, they propose UFP4, a recipe built on uniform E1M2/INT4 grids that applies RHT to all three training GEMMs and limits stochastic rounding to the activation gradient. UFP4 is evaluated on Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B pretraining runs with scaling-law analysis and ablations, consistently outperforming E2M1 baselines on BF16-relative loss degradation. The paper concludes with a hardware recommendation: future accelerator designs should support uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1.
novelty: Geometric analysis of shrinkage bias in E2M1 FP4 format; demonstration that uniform 4-bit grids (E1M2/INT4) outperform non-uniform formats for LLM pretraining stability and quality.
audience: LLM infrastructure engineers and accelerator architects designing efficient 4-bit training systems; researchers optimizing quantization methods for production pretraining.

title: The FID Lottery: Quantifying Hidden Randomness in Generative-Model Evaluation
why: Single FID numbers reported in generative-model papers are statistically unreliable, and this paper provides the empirical framework to know by how much, which invalidates a large fraction of published comparisons.
notes: Retraining a model with a different seed moves FID 3.2x farther in Inception feature space than resampling from a fixed, already-trained network, making training-seed variance the dominant source of noise in reported scores.
Three factors drive training-seed variance: random weight initialization, data-ordering across epochs, and the per-step Gaussian noise injected by the flow-matching loss during training.
FID coefficient of variation stays within a 1-2% band regardless of compute budget or model scale, so simply running larger experiments does not resolve the reproducibility problem.

Per-cell classifier-free guidance tuning halves the variance spread but reshuffles which training seeds rank best, meaning guidance-tuned comparisons still require multiple seeds; a lucky seed achieves the same FID with up to 2x less compute than an unlucky one.

The study spans several hundred SiT networks trained on class-conditional ImageNet 256x256; the authors recommend treating any FID gap below approximately 1.3% CoV as inconclusive and mandating error bars over multiple training seeds rather than a single reported number.
summary: This paper treats FID as a random variable decomposed along two axes, training seed and sampling seed, and measures its variance empirically across several hundred SiT diffusion networks trained on class-conditional ImageNet 256x256. The central finding is that variance from retraining with a different random seed is 3.2 times larger (in Inception feature space) than variance from resampling a fixed network, attributable to random initialization, data ordering, and per-step Gaussian noise in the flow-matching loss. The FID coefficient of variation holds within a 1-2% band regardless of compute or model size, indicating that scaling does not tighten the spread. Per-cell classifier-free guidance tuning halves variance but reshuffles which seeds produce the best scores, and a lucky training seed can match a given FID target at up to 2x lower compute cost than an unlucky one. The authors propose a revised evaluation protocol: apply per-cell optimal guidance, treat any FID gap smaller than roughly 1.3% CoV as statistically inconclusive, and report error bars computed across multiple training seeds rather than a single number from a single run. The practical implication is that many claimed improvements in the image generation literature fall below the noise floor of the metric used to establish them.
novelty: Quantifies that FID variance from retraining dominates sampling variance by 3.2x, revealing that current single-number reporting obscures reproducibility; proposes error-bar protocol over training seeds.
audience: Image generation researchers and practitioners who use FID for evaluation; anyone comparing generative model papers and implicitly trusting reported numbers.

title: To study how chips work, MIT researchers built their own operating system
why: Fractal delivers the first controlled evidence that Apple Silicon is vulnerable to Phantom speculation and that M1's CSV2 privilege isolation leaks through the instruction cache, findings that were invisible to prior researchers precisely because macOS was contaminating their measurements.
notes: Fractal is a bare-metal OS kernel (~31,000 lines, x86_64/ARM64/RISC-V) built around 'multi-privilege concurrency': a single experiment switches privilege levels at runtime while holding address space and instructions constant, eliminating scheduler, interrupt, and ASLR noise that blurs macOS and Linux baselines.

Apple M1's CSV2 execute-phase protection for indirect branch prediction holds as documented, but the CPU still fetches the speculated target into the instruction cache before the protection check completes; that fetch is observable as a side channel, so user-mode code can influence kernel cache state across the privilege boundary.

Fractal produces the first confirmed instance of Phantom speculation on Apple Silicon: ordinary instructions, including no-ops, are misinterpreted as branches and trigger speculative fetches across both privilege levels and address-space identifiers (ASIDs); the execute phase remains blocked, but the fetch side channel survives.

A prior publication had claimed M1's conditional branch predictor isolates cross-privilege training on efficiency cores but not performance cores; Fractal shows neither core type has any privilege isolation, and the earlier null result was likely an artifact of macOS silently migrating threads between cores during system calls.

MIT disclosed the M1 findings to Apple's product security team; Apple's engineers examined Fractal in return. The work was presented at IEEE S&P 2026; funding includes NSF, AFOSR, and DARPA's ACE program.
summary: MIT CSAIL PhD student Joseph Ravichandran and associate professor Mengjia Yan developed Fractal, a bare-metal OS kernel purpose-built for microarchitecture experimentation, to eliminate the measurement noise introduced when researchers patch general-purpose kernels like macOS or Linux. Its key abstraction, multi-privilege concurrency, lets a single experiment execute identical instructions at different CPU privilege levels within the same address space, isolating privilege as a true independent variable. Applied to Apple's M1, Fractal confirmed that the ARM CSV2 speculative-isolation mechanism blocks cross-privilege indirect-branch execution but found that the processor fetches speculated targets into the instruction cache before the protection activates, leaving a timing side channel across both privilege and ASID boundaries. The same experiments produced the first documented evidence of Phantom speculation on Apple Silicon, a class of misprediction in which non-branch instructions trigger speculative fetches, previously demonstrated only on AMD and Intel hardware. Fractal also overturned an earlier finding on M1's conditional branch predictor, attributing the prior discrepancy to macOS thread migration rather than genuine hardware isolation. The team intends Fractal as shared community infrastructure, noting POSIX compatibility and ports of standard tools, and disclosed all M1 findings to Apple, whose security engineers subsequently reviewed the kernel.
novelty: Fractal as reusable research infrastructure for microarchitecture measurement + first Phantom speculation evidence on Apple Silicon + CSV2 protection gaps via instruction-cache side channel + conditional branch predictor isolation failure on M1
audience: Security researchers and hardware engineers studying speculative execution vulnerabilities and microarchitecture behavior

title: W Social, public institutions and the theater of European digital sovereignty
why: EU institutions appear to have migrated their federated social presence to a platform that quietly went closed-source, days after Brussels announced an open-source digital sovereignty mandate for public administrations.
notes: On June 12, the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, the ECB, and Christine Lagarde moved their ATproto accounts from Bluesky PBC to W Social, confirmed via clearsky.app; the migration came one week after the EU Tech Sovereignty Package explicitly committed public administrations to open-source alternatives.

W Social's GitHub organization (github.com/w-social-eu) was deleted with no notice sometime around March 2026; the author found no replacement repository on GitHub, Codeberg, or other platforms; Aral Balkan (Small Technology Foundation) notes the standard practice is to deprecate and redirect, not delete, calling the removal 'highly unusual'.

W Social has not publicly disclosed whether it self-hosts all five ATproto stack components on European infrastructure; a full sovereign deployment requires PDS, relay, AppView, moderation service, and PLC directory, and the company's silence on the latter four makes its 'European digital sovereignty' claim unverifiable.

W Social's advisory board includes Marc Placzek, currently Chief Privacy Officer at Tools for Humanity (the iris-scan global-ID company co-founded by Sam Altman), and Yariv Adan, former AI lead at Alphabet; W Social requires identity verification as a core platform feature.

Eurosky, operated by the non-profit Modal Foundation, offers a published development roadmap, independently mirrored did:plc directory, own ATproto firehose on European infrastructure, and a full Bluesky-app replacement (mu.social); neither EU body chose it. This article is presented by its author as opinion and independent research, not established fact.
summary: Independent researcher Elena Rossini reports, apparently as a first, that W Social's public GitHub repository was silently deleted around March 2026, converting the platform from open-source to an undisclosed codebase without notice. One week after the EU announced a Tech Sovereignty Package requiring open-source adoption in public administrations, the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, the ECB, and Christine Lagarde migrated their ATproto accounts from Bluesky PBC to W Social, a for-profit Swedish private company. W Social has not disclosed whether it independently operates the relay, AppView, moderation service, and PLC directory components of the ATproto stack, or remains dependent on Bluesky PBC infrastructure for those services, leaving its sovereignty claims unsubstantiated. Advisory board connections -- notably Marc Placzek's concurrent role as CPO of Tools for Humanity, the Worldcoin biometric identity platform -- raise questions about the company's alignment with public-interest digital infrastructure, particularly given W Social's mandatory identity verification requirement. The Modal Foundation's Eurosky project provides a non-profit, fully transparent ATproto alternative with independent European infrastructure, and was not selected by either institution. Rossini frames this as a pattern of institutional due diligence failures in EU digital policy, and the piece is presented as opinion and independent commentary rather than verified reporting.
novelty: Closed-source pivot contradicts EU's open-source Tech Sovereignty Package; first reporting of W Social GitHub deletion and EU institutional account migration
audience: EU technologists and policy observers tracking digital sovereignty implementation; security researchers monitoring surveillance capitalism adoption by institutions

title: ImageWAM: Do World Action Models Really Need Video Generation, or Just Image Editing?
why: If robot world-action models can match or beat video-generation baselines at 1/6 the FLOPs by substituting image-editing KV caches, the field's default architectural choice is load-bearing overhead rather than principled design.
notes: Video-based WAMs carry three coupled costs the paper identifies: dense multi-frame token sequences inflate inference cost, full video prediction spends model capacity on action-irrelevant temporal and appearance details, and long-horizon future rollouts accumulate errors that compound into misleading action signals.

ImageWAM conditions a flow-matching action expert on KV caches produced during image-editing denoising; the target frame is never decoded at inference, so the world representation stays a compact latent rather than an explicit pixel prediction.

Reported efficiency gains are 1/6 FLOPs and 1/4 latency versus video-based WAM baselines, measured across simulator and real-world robot experiments; no additional policy pretraining was required to match or exceed VLA baselines and competitive WAMs.

Attention analysis of the editing KV caches shows concentration on task-relevant visual change regions rather than static background, which the authors cite as mechanistic evidence that edit priors are more action-aligned than video generation priors.

Core framing: image editing models a current-to-target-frame transformation, focusing only on what changes due to the action, whereas video generation must also model action-irrelevant temporal continuity and appearance consistency across intermediate frames.
summary: ImageWAM questions the standard assumption that robot World Action Models require video generation as their world-modeling backbone. The paper argues that pretrained image editing models offer a better-matched prior for action prediction: they model only the current-to-target-frame transformation, concentrating capacity on action-relevant visual differences and avoiding the compounding errors of multi-step future rollouts. At inference, ImageWAM extracts KV caches from an image-editing denoiser and uses them to condition a flow-matching action expert, never decoding an explicit target frame. On simulator and real-world benchmarks, the system matches or exceeds competing WAMs and VLA baselines without additional policy pretraining, at claimed reductions of 1/6 FLOPs and 1/4 latency relative to video-based counterparts. Attention visualizations showing cache concentration on task-relevant change regions provide mechanistic support for the core hypothesis. The result is primarily a structural argument: if the bottleneck for robot control is identifying action-relevant visual change rather than predicting plausible video, video generation may be the wrong inductive bias entirely.
novelty: Challenges video generation as necessity for world action models; repurposes image-editing model KV caches as efficient, action-focused alternative with 6x FLOP and 4x latency reductions
audience: ML researchers and roboticists building embodied agents; engineers optimizing inference efficiency in world models

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
why: The iframe-plus-CSP-meta-header sandboxing pattern Willison documents here, and the privilege-escalation class of bug it surfaced, are directly reusable for anyone building hosted AI artifacts or user-generated apps on an authenticated domain.
notes: The core isolation relies on combining `<iframe sandbox="allow-scripts allow-forms" srcdoc="...">` with a `<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy">` injected into the frame content; Willison notes the CSP is immutable once set within the frame, so JavaScript inside cannot relax it to reach external hosts.

Cross-frame communication uses `MessageChannel()` rather than bare `postMessage()` as a defense-in-depth measure: the channel closes automatically if the iframe navigates away, eliminating the risk of stale handlers accepting commands from an untrusted external page; Willison credits a GPT-5.5 suggestion for the switch, while noting he did not confirm the postMessage attack applied in this specific context.

Write operations are exposed only through named stored queries (formerly "canned queries", upgraded in Datasette 1.0a31) that must be explicitly allow-listed per app, so the iframe calls `datasette.storedQuery("todos", "add_todo", {...})` but cannot issue freeform SQL writes.

A real privilege-escalation vulnerability was identified during a Claude Fable 5 security evaluation: a low-privilege user with `create-app` permission could build an app that exfiltrates data to a CSP-allow-listed external host, then social-engineer an admin into visiting it; fixed by gating CSP domain allow-listing behind a new `apps-set-csp` permission, with site admins able to pre-configure an `allowed_csp_origins` list for regular users to select from.

The plugin itself has no LLM dependency; development used Claude Opus 4.6 in Claude Code for initial prototyping and GPT-5.5 xhigh via Codex Desktop for planning, but AI is only an optional authoring path for end users via a bundled copyable prompt that includes the target database schema.
summary: Simon Willison launched `datasette-apps`, a Datasette plugin that hosts self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside tightly sandboxed iframes, allowing read-only SQL queries against Datasette databases and optional write operations via named stored queries. The isolation mechanism combines `<iframe sandbox="allow-scripts allow-forms" srcdoc="...">` with a `<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy">` header injected into the frame, which blocks external HTTP requests and, per Willison's testing, cannot be modified or removed by JavaScript once set. Cross-frame communication uses `MessageChannel()` rather than raw `postMessage()` so that the command channel closes automatically on iframe navigation, removing a potential attack surface for externally injected code. A privilege-escalation vulnerability, identified during a Claude Fable 5 security evaluation, showed that a low-privilege user with `create-app` permission could craft an app to exfiltrate an admin user's private data to a CSP-allow-listed domain if the admin visited the app; the fix restricts CSP domain allow-listing to a new `apps-set-csp` permission and lets site admins pre-configure an `allowed_csp_origins` list. The plugin carries no LLM dependency but ships a copyable prompt containing the target database schema so users can generate compatible app code in any external model.
novelty: iframe + CSP hardening pattern for untrusted code; practical privilege-escalation vulnerability (admin-tricking) in app sandboxing discovered and fixed via permission model
audience: Systems engineers building dev tools; developers working on data infrastructure and safe code sandboxing patterns

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
why: Every major coding-LLM leaderboard is Python-only; this benchmark makes the cost of that blind spot measurable by extending LiveCodeBench to eleven additional languages with the same contamination controls already trusted by the field.
notes: Multi-LCB covers 12 programming languages (Python plus 11 others, unspecified in the abstract) by mechanically transforming existing LCB competitive-programming tasks, preserving LCB's release-date-based contamination filtering rather than constructing a new problem set.

24 instruction-tuned and reasoning LLMs were evaluated; full format compatibility with the original LCB schema means the benchmark automatically ingests future LCB problem additions without manual curation.

The paper reports evidence of Python overfitting: current LLMs trained at scale show substantially lower performance on non-Python tasks relative to their Python baselines, though specific delta magnitudes are not disclosed in the abstract.

Language-specific contamination was detected, meaning training-data leakage is not uniform across target languages; this complicates per-language score interpretation and suggests some non-Python scores may be inflated by data overlap.

Caveat: the abstract does not name the 12 languages, identify which of the 24 models showed the largest gaps, or quantify the performance disparities numerically; claims should be treated as pending the full paper's tables.
summary: Multi-LCB is a multilingual code-generation benchmark that extends LiveCodeBench (LCB) from Python-only to twelve programming languages by transforming existing LCB competitive-programming tasks into semantically equivalent tasks in each target language, while preserving LCB's contamination-aware evaluation protocol based on problem release dates. Because it is fully format-compatible with the original LCB dataset, Multi-LCB will automatically incorporate future LCB problem additions, keeping the benchmark current without separate curation effort. An evaluation of 24 instruction-tuned and reasoning LLMs surfaces three findings: evidence of Python overfitting in current models, language-specific variation in training-data contamination that affects per-language score validity, and substantial but unquantified performance disparities across the twelve languages. The authors argue that Python-only benchmarks have concealed critical generalization gaps in LLM coding capability, and position Multi-LCB as a drop-in replacement that directly addresses LCB's primary limitation. The abstract does not disclose which languages are included beyond Python, which specific models exhibit the largest regressions, or the numerical magnitude of the performance gaps; those details require reading the full paper.
novelty: Multilingual extension of LiveCodeBench from Python-only to 12 languages; empirical quantification of Python overfitting and language-specific contamination in LLM training data.
audience: ML researchers and teams evaluating LLM code generation capabilities across diverse programming languages; practitioners building multilingual code tools.

title: Google workspace threatening to block firefox access
why: Google is now using enterprise security policy machinery to steer Workspace users toward Chrome, which matters to any team that ships browser-compatible software or administers Google Workspace seats.
notes: As of 2026-06-18, Google Workspace Business Plus began surfacing a warning to Firefox users at access.workspace.google.com/remediate stating the device fails unspecified 'organisation security requirements'; the only prescribed remedy is installing Chrome.
The security framing appears pretextual: the reporter was running a fully up-to-date Firefox and OS, and the warning provides no explanation of which requirement is unmet.
Firefox access was still functional at time of publication; the warning is advisory-only for now, but the UI implies impending enforcement with no stated deadline.
This is a single Business Plus account report; there is no official Google statement on scope, rollout timeline, or whether other Workspace tiers are affected.

The mechanism leverages enterprise device-compliance flows (the /remediate endpoint) rather than a traditional browser-sniffing block, framing lock-in as a security posture rather than a product decision.
summary: On 2026-06-18, a Google Workspace Business Plus user reported receiving a warning in Firefox that their device fails organizational security requirements, with Chrome download listed as the sole remediation step, served via access.workspace.google.com/remediate. The security justification is opaque: the user's browser and OS were fully updated, and the warning names no specific unmet policy. Firefox remains functional as of the report, but the warning UI implies enforcement is forthcoming. The mechanism routes through enterprise device-compliance infrastructure rather than a simple browser check, giving Google cover to frame the restriction as a security posture. The report is limited to a single Business Plus account, and Google has issued no public statement on policy scope or rollout timeline. The move signals a shift from Google's historical passive neglect of Firefox compatibility in Workspace toward active, policy-enforced pressure toward Chrome.
novelty: Google escalating from passive Firefox neglect to active blocking/warning in Workspace, using security pretexts for browser lock-in
audience: Development teams using Google Workspace who require cross-browser testing; engineers concerned about vendor enforcement of browser choice
